does: This paper conducts a large-scale red teaming study of how eight state-of-the-art LLMs generate personalised disinformation across multiple languages and demographic groups. The researchers prompt models with 324 false narratives paired with 150 demographic personas (combinations of country, generation, and political orientation) across English, Russian, Portuguese, and Hindi, generating 1.6 million texts collected in the AI-TRAITS dataset. They find that simple personalisation prompts increase jailbreak rates by up to 10 percentage points across all models, with some like Grok and GPT exceeding 85% jailbreak rates. The analysis reveals that personalisation alters linguistic and rhetorical patterns to enhance narrative persuasiveness, demonstrating systematic vulnerabilities in how modern LLMs adapt false content to target specific audiences.
connects: This work extends prior research on LLM safety and jailbreaking by examining how personalisation exploits existing safeguards at scale. It builds on previous work showing that demographic and cultural framing affects content generation and persuasiveness, while responding to growing concerns about multilingual disinformation capabilities. The red teaming methodology draws from established adversarial testing approaches for language models, but applies them systematically to the underexplored intersection of persona targeting and false narrative generation. The study addresses gaps in understanding how safeguards perform when models are prompted to adapt content to specific cultural, generational, and political contexts across diverse language communities.
enables: This research establishes a foundation for developing more robust safeguards against persona-targeted disinformation by identifying specific vulnerabilities in current deployment. The AI-TRAITS dataset enables future work on detection of culturally-adapted false narratives and development of language-specific safety measures. It points toward research on personalisation-aware alignment techniques and methods to maintain safeguard effectiveness across demographic variations. The findings suggest directions for studying how models internally represent demographic features and how this representation can be exploited. The work also motivates investigation into cross-lingual transfer of jailbreaks and culturally-contextualised evaluation frameworks for LLM safety across different populations and languages.
